data.title: Copper prices head of weekly gain on US-China tariff truce
data.description:
Copper prices edged lower on Friday but were set for a weekly rise on China-U.S. temporary trade truce, although uncertainty over longer-term demand prospects for the metal capped further gains.

Benchmark copper on the London Metal Exchange (LME) was down 0.4% at $9,536 a metric ton, as of 0707 GMT. The most-traded copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) eased 0.3% to 78,140 yuan ($10,851.12) per ton.

For the week, LME copper is up about 1.1%, while prices in Shanghai have risen around 0.6%.

"Chinese traders are happy about the 90-day pause, but the market has remained uncertain about what is going to happen after the 90 days," a Shanghai-based metals analyst said.

"The fact that China's exporters have been rushing cargoes out is a tell-tale sign."

The initial optimism about the 90-day pause agreed by Beijing and Washington on most of their retaliatory tariffs has faded. The market is now focusing on the potential new tariffs on copper imports that the U.S. has been considering since February.

Chinese analysts expect the SHFE copper price to hover around 78,000–79,000 yuan per ton in the near term, reflecting mixed market sentiment.

Copper stocks in the SHFE-monitored warehouses surged 34% on week to 108,142 tons.

"It is not surprising to see the copper stocks up, just that the pace may be a bit too fast, which may add pressure on copper prices in the near term," the Shanghai-based analyst said.

Among other London metals, aluminium slipped 0.8% to $2,468.5 per ton, zinc fell 1.1% to $2,694.5, lead lost 1.1% to $1,983 and tin edged down 0.5% to $32,800.

SHFE aluminium fell 0.5% to 20,130 yuan a ton, zinc dropped 1.1% to 22,500 yuan and lead slipped 0.8% to 16,870 yuan.

($1 = 7.2011 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Tonner Drones CEO Boosts Stake to 13% with Warrants Exercise
data.description:
Tonner Drones Chief Executive Officer Van den Ouden raised his stake in the company to 13% after converting 30,312,000 warrants into 45,468,000 regular shares for 568,350 euros.

The French logistics sector-focused technologies developer said Friday that Ouden now holds 65,468,000 shares and zero warrants.

Ouden now holds a regular bond of 3 million euros with a 3% interest, maturing on Dec. 12, 2027, and a 1.5 million-euro shareholder loan with 5% interest.

data.title: WeRide Launches Fully Autonomous Taxi Trials in Abu Dhabi
data.description:
WeRide said Friday it has launched fully driverless robo-taxi trial operations in Abu Dhabi, the capital of the United Arab Emirates.

Beginning Q2, a fleet of WeRide's autonomous taxis will begin operating on public roads without a safety driver, the company said.

WeRide also said it will expand service coverage to two new districts of Abu Dhabi.

Commercial autonomous vehicle rides and more service areas will be introduced beginning summer 2025, subject to regulatory approval, the company said.

WeRide shares were 4% higher pre-bell.

data.title: xSuite Nordic Invites Customers to the 2025 User Conference in Copenhagen
data.description:
Under the Theme "One Team. One Journey," the Software Provider Showcases Innovations and Solutions for the Finance Sector

Copenhagen/Denmark, May 16, 2025 – xSuite Nordic cordially invites users and partners to its 2025 User Conference on June 12 in Copenhagen, offering an immersive experience into future-ready technologies. This one-day event will bring together IT and finance professionals to explore key developments in invoice processing, artificial intelligence, SAP S/4HANA, cloud computing, and SAP Clean Core strategies.

Attendees will gain valuable insights into how technologies such as AI and cloud platforms are accelerating digital transformation and opening new opportunities in finance. xSuite will present its latest product innovations, share its strategic roadmap, and provide a glimpse into emerging tech trends that are reshaping the financial landscape.

Program Highlights

1. Deep Dive: Artificial Intelligence – Discover how xSuite’s Prediction Server delivers AI-powered support for invoice processing within SAP environments. This session will demonstrate how AI is extending its reach across financial workflows and how Large Language Models (LLMs) are revolutionizing document recognition and data extraction.

2. Deep Dive: SAP S/4HANA and Cloud – With many organizations advancing their SAP S/4HANA migration, aligning with SAP’s Clean Core strategy is becoming critical—even within Private Cloud infrastructures—to avoid future technical debt. This session will provide insights into xSuite’s modern solution architecture, including SAP-integrated Business Solutions 6.0 and applications built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

The conference will conclude with networking opportunities and open discussions about customer requirements, highlighting xSuite’s role as a trusted partner in digital transformation.

Event Details:

xSuite User Conference

Date: June 12, 2025

Location: Danish Architecture Center  | Bryghuspladsen 10 | 1473 Copenhagen

Time: 09:00 AM – 3:00 PM

More information and registration: xSuite User Conference 2025 in Copenhagen

About xSuite Group

xSuite is a software manufacturer of applications for document-based processes and provides standardized, digital solutions worldwide that enable simple, secure, and fast work. We focus mainly on the automation of important work processes in conjunction with end-to-end document management. Our core competence lies in accounts payable (AP) automation in SAP (including

e-invoicing), for leading companies worldwide, as well as for public clients. This is supplemented by applications for purchasing and order processes as well as archiving – all delivered from a single source, including both software components and services. xSuite solutions operate in the cloud or in hybrid scenarios. We take pride in the high-quality solutions we offer, as evidenced by the regular certifications we receive for our SAP solutions and deployment environments." With over 300,000 users benefitting from our solutions, xSuite processes more than 80 million documents per year in over 60 countries.

Founded in 1994 and headquartered in Ahrensburg, Germany, xSuite has around 300 staff across nine locations worldwide – in Europe, Asia, and the United States. Our company has an established information security management system that is certified in accordance with ISO 27001:2022.

data.title: Volkswagen Expects Auto Industry to Remain Challenging
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Volkswagen expects conditions in the auto industry to remain challenging going forward, as it noted the operating environment is "strewn with massive risks" created by external factors.

In a speech manuscript released in conjunction with the company's annual shareholder meeting Friday, the company's Chief Executive Oliver Blume said the group must take action to ensure its cost base is more robust as the coming years will determine who will play a role in the automotive world of the future.

"We must consistently build up our product advantages and continue to work with discipline on a competitive cost structure," he said.

On top of continuing challenges in China, where intense competition has triggered a price war, President Trump's tariff regime has thrown the industry into further turmoil as cars and parts imported to the U.S. now face hefty duties.

Blume expects 2025 will be another demanding year in China, with continued competitive pressure. However, the German auto group is continuing with its strategy of developing cars in China fully tailored to local customers and plans extensive model launches in the country from 2026.

In North America, the company saw deliveries grow over 6% last year and it is aiming for further growth in the region, despite the challenges surrounding current trade policy.

"North America remains a key element in the Volkswagen Group's growth strategy," Blume said. "We believe this region has significant potential."

He said the company is formulating a vision for North America, with a clear plan of measures and responsibilities, including products that are geared to American customers.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com

data.title: Lecron Industrial Development Recoups 2.6 Million Yuan in Fraud Case Compensation
data.description:
Lecron Industrial Development Group received 2.6 million yuan in court-ordered restitution from a 2022 contract fraud case involving five convicted executives.

The payment follows final judgments against former officers, including life imprisonment and confiscation of all personal property for Kong Gang as well as 15-year sentence and 2-million-yuan fine for Gao Shengning, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

data.title: Turkey's Dogus opens first Hyatt hotel in Croatia
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), May 16 (SeeNews) - Turkish holding company Dogus opened Hyatt Regency Zadar, the first hotel under the Hyatt brand in Croatia, Hyatt Hotels Corporation said.

Hyatt Regency Zadar offers 133 guestrooms and suites, food and beverage venues, more than 740 square metres of event space, and a 1,155-square-meter spa, Hyatt Hotels Corporation said in a press release on Thursday.

The hotel in Zadar showcases the company’s progress toward expanding the Hyatt Regency brand by 70% on the Balkan Peninsula by 2027, the company said.

"The Balkans is a priority growth area for us, and a destination we are seeing grow in popularity for business and leisure travelers looking to experience both beautiful landscapes and rich cultural heritage," Michel Morauw, managing director at Hyatt, said.

Dogus invested 55 million euro ($61.6 million) in the construction of the hotel in Zadar, according to an earlier statement by Hyatt Regency Zadar.

($ = 0.893 euro)

data.title: Malvern International Extends Services Contract with UK University
data.description:
Malvern International secured a new one-year contract with the University of East London to continue providing international student recruitment, teaching and support services for its International Study Centre during the 2025-2026 academic year.

The British learning and skills development company is also negotiating a multi-year agreement with the university, with a deal expected within 2025, according to a Friday release.

data.title: Cartier Parent Richemont Posts Higher Sales on Jewelry Boost — Update
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras

Swiss jeweler and watchmaker Richemont reported an increase in sales for its fiscal fourth quarter boosted by its core jewelry division, dodging a demand slowdown that is plaguing the luxury sector and a global trade spat.

High-end brands are gearing up for the impact of U.S. President Trump's tariffs which come at a time the sector is contending with a wider downturn in demand in key markets, particularly China, where economic challenges have prompted shoppers to cut back on luxury purchases.

The owner of brands such as Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels is looking at options including price increases in response to trade tariffs, Finance Chief Burkhart Grund said Friday.

"We are closely monitoring the situation," Grund said in an earnings call.

Richemont generated sales of 5.17 billion euros ($5.78 billion) for the last three months of its fiscal year ended March 31. This was 7% higher than in the prior-year period at constant exchange rates. The result compares with analysts' expectations of 5.14 billion euros, according to a poll of estimates compiled by Visible Alpha.

The group's core jewelry division recorded sales of 3.74 billion euros, surpassing analysts' views of 3.68 billion euros, according to the same consensus. Higher sales, combined with measures including price increases, helped the company mitigate the impact of higher costs of raw materials, notably gold, it said.

Shares jumped 5.2% in European morning trading. Since the start of 2025, they are up more than 18%.

Richemont reported a strong end to its fiscal year and continued to gain market share in jewelry, Vontobel analyst Jean-Philippe Bertschy said in a note. "Growth and profit are spectacular, especially when comparing to key competitor LVMH," the analyst said.

Despite Richemont's strong position, the group is not immune to the current volatile environment, Bertschy added.

The watch segment recorded sales of 759 million euros, 11% lower on year at constant rates, due to its high exposure to China and a slowdown in the global watch market.

China will be back once consumers get a little more confident, Richemont Chairman Johann Rupert said. "It helps to fly with five engines. If one engine goes down, you still have four," Rupert said, referring to the company's strong performance in other regions.

In the Americas, which includes the key U.S. market, the group saw a 16% increase in fourth-quarter sales at constant rates. Most luxury brands recorded growth in the U.S. in the last quarter of 2024, raising hopes of a pickup in demand for high-end goods after a post-pandemic euphoria gave way to a slowdown.

Richemont continues to see very good traction in the Americas, and that strong performance is likely to continue, Chief Executive Nicolas Bos said.

The unknown outcome around Trump's tariffs and global economic uncertainty were seen as a potential hit to demand for pricey handbags, fashion and accessories. In April, Birkin bag maker Hermes said it will lift prices to mitigate the potential impact from levies in the U.S.

Looking ahead, Richemont said it is well-positioned for its next phase of development, but that the current global uncertainties require agility and discipline.

Net profit from continuing operations fell to 3.76 billion euros from 3.82 billion euros previously.

Richemont proposed a dividend of 3.00 Swiss francs a share, compared with 2.75 Swiss francs in the prior fiscal year.

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

data.title: India's Jubilant Pharmova hits 3-week high on strong Q4 results
data.description:
** Jubilant Pharmova rises 4% to 942 rupees, highest in three weeks

** Drugmaker posts Q4 consol net profit of 1.54 bln rupees ($18 mln) vs year-ago loss of 586 mln rupees, which included a one time charge of 1.69 bln rupees

** Still, profit before one-time charge up 82% y/y

** Rev rises 10% y/y, driven by strong demand for co's drugs with radioactive ingredients

** JUBA was up 0.3% before results

** Volumes pick up post results, with shares traded ~3x the 30-day avg

** YTD, JUBA falls 18%

data.title: Thomas Cook (India) Opens New Office in Cyprus
data.description:
Travel services provider Thomas Cook (India) has established its presence in the European Union by setting up its first office in Cyprus, according to a Friday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The office will be operated by the company's subsidiary Travel Circle International (Cyprus), which will focus on the business travel needs of its multinational clients and India-based clients with a presence in Europe.

data.title: Malaysia's Weaker Economic Growth Could Prompt Rate Cuts — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's economy is likely to struggle this year, opening the door for the central bank to cut interest rates, says Gareth Leather, senior Asia economist at Capital Economics in a note. Although Malaysia should continue gaining from the data center construction boom, Capital Economics reckons that investment will weaken, as firms hold back on expansion plans due to uncertainty on tariffs and global trade tensions. With growth set to struggle, there could be more pressure for the central bank to cut rates, Leather says. Capital Economics has penciled in two 25bps rate cuts this year from the central bank and expects Malaysia's 2025 GDP growth to be at 4.8%, down from 2024's 5.1%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Italian Annual Inflation Rate Holds Steady at 1.9% in April, Final Data Shows
data.description:
Italy's annual inflation rate came in at 1.9% in April, unchanged from March, final data from the statistical office Istat showed Friday.

The flash estimate for the month was 2%.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices were 0.1% higher, compared with the preliminary reading of a 0.2% gain and the prior 0.3% increase.

The Italian harmonized inflation rate was 2% on an annual basis, down from the prior and initial reading of 2.1%. Month over month, harmonized consumer prices increased 0.4%, against the 1.6% rise earlier and the flash figure of a 0.5% gain.

data.title: Hang Seng Slips Further But Surges 2% for the Week
data.description:
The Hang Seng dropped 108 points or 0.5% to end at 23,345 on Friday, down for the second day amid broad-based losses across all sectors.

Investors were unsure how policies would evolve when the 90-day trade war truce between Washington and Beijing ends in July.

A climb in US futures failed to lift sentiment as traders were on edge ahead of China’s key April data next week, including industrial output and retail sales.

Meanwhile, the PBoC is set to review its benchmark lending rates, which have stayed at record lows in recent months as the economy faces domestic and external pressures.

Still, the Hang Seng rose about 2% for the week—its fifth straight weekly gain and the longest run since February—boosted by upgraded growth forecasts for China from some global investment banks, on bets of continued front-loading of exports to the U.S. Alibaba slid 3.9% after missing revenue estimates, while Hang Seng Bank (-3.3%), Swire Pacific (-3.2%), and Meituan (-3.1%) also posted steep losses.

data.title: Capital flows back to US-listed China ETFs post big sell-off
data.description:
Major U.S.-listed exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that track Chinese stocks recorded inflows in May as a U.S.-China tariff truce boosted sentiment, following a heavy sell-off in the previous month.

BY THE NUMBERS

Global investors bought a combined $401.7 million in four major U.S.-listed China ETFs - iShares MSCI China ETF , iShares China Large-Cap ETF , KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF and Xtrackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares ETF this month through May 15.

That compared with a $3.8 billion outflow in April, data from LSEG Lipper showed.

April marked the second largest outflow on Lipper's record, second only to a $4.4 billion outflow in November 2024.

U.S. institutional investors currently own about $250 billion in U.S.-listed Chinese stocks, according to Goldman Sachs.

WHY IT'S IMPORTANT

Analysts are closely watching flows into Chinese shares traded in U.S. markets to gauge the extent of investor concern over the potential removal of Chinese companies from U.S stock exchanges - a delisting that could heighten the financial decoupling between the two biggest economies.

Those concerns were at the fore in April when U.S. President Donald Trump dramatically escalated his trade war with China by raising tariffs on Chinese imports to 145%, while U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent hinted the possible delisting of Chinese stocks from U.S. bourses might figure in trade talks.

KEY QUOTES

"The selling pressure in April was mainly due to trade tensions ... we have seen some sentiment recovery in May," said Jason Lui, head of APAC equity and derivative strategy at BNP Paribas.

"The majority of outflows in April came from hedge fund and arbitrage strategy players. We see most institutions stay invested in our fund," said Xiaolin Chen, head of international at KraneShares, which manages the $7 billion KWEB.

data.title: Market Chatter: Resonac Holdings to Exit China, Southeast Asia Graphite Electrode Production to Cut Losses
data.description:
Resonac Holdings will exit graphite electrode production in China and Southeast Asia to improve margins amid a glut of cheap Chinese-made parts, Nikkei Asia reported Friday citing Chief Financial Officer Hideki Somemiya.

The company will liquidate subsidiaries in China and Malaysia, ceasing operations at two plants that, along with a now-closed third site, accounted for roughly one-third of its global electrode output, the report noted.

Resonac will continue producing graphite electrodes - key components for electric arc furnaces - at four locations in Japan, the US, Austria and Spain, Nikkei reported.

The decision follows deepening losses in the company's chemicals segment, which includes electrodes. Core operating loss for the chemicals segment widened to 6.3 billion yen in the January-March quarter, compared to a loss of 800 million yen a year earlier.

CFO Somemiya said the company will continue to assess its global manufacturing footprint and will outline additional restructuring steps around August, aiming to return to profitability in the second half of fiscal 2025, Nikkei reported.

data.title: Chinese Stocks Slip as Tariff Pause Optimism Fades
data.description:
Chinese stocks dipped on Friday as initial optimism regarding the tariff truce between the US and China started to wane.

The Shanghai Composite Index, the main gauge of Chinese stocks, slid 0.4% or by 13.64 points to 3,367.18. The Shenzhen Component Index declined marginally by 1.53 points to 10,184.92.

As investors are seeking cues from US President Donald Trump to see the progress of talks with the country's trading partners, the optimism surrounding the ceasefire has been losing momentum due to remnants of high tariffs, the Times of India reported Friday.

Even before the tariff pause, China had already started diversifying its exports away from the US to ensure it would never again be vulnerable to Washington's trade actions, according to Bloomberg.

China will have to work with other countries to open markets for goods originally intended for US consumers if it wants to reduce dependence on US shipments even further, Bloomberg's Allen Wan said.

In the corporate front, Shenghe Resources closed 4% lower after subsidiary Chenguang Rare Earth agreed to fully acquire Australia's Peak Rare Earths for AU$158 million.

data.title: Global Markets Mixed as U.S.-China Trade Truce Boost Loses Momentum
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

Global stocks were mixed and U.S. stock futures pointed to an unchanged open after a strong start to the week that was boosted by the U.S.-China trade truce.

The dollar and Treasury yields eased early Friday in Europe as money markets continued to bet on interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve after this week's soft CPI print. April housing starts data land later.

  • U.S. Treasury yields dropped in early European trade, extending Thursday's trend. The two- and 10-year Treasury yields were last down around 3 basis points each to 3.944% and 4.421%, respectively, according to LSEG data.
  • The dollar was lower after lower-than-expected U.S. consumer prices data and persistent pressure on oil prices added to the sense that inflationary pressures are easing, Deutsche Bank analysts said in a note. The DXY dollar index was last down 0.3% to 100.576.
  • U.S. stock futures were trading flat across the board. Asian shares ended mixed. Japan's Nikkei fluctuated between positive and negative territory after first-quarter GDP data revealed that Japan's economy contracted for the first time in a year. South Korea's Kospi closed 0.2% higher. In China, shares were mixed as focus shifts to industrial output and retail sales data on Monday.
  • European stock markets rose at the open. The Stoxx Europe 600 climbed 0.5%. Other stocks in Europe were up as France's CAC 40 gained 0.4% and Germany's DAX rose 0.5%. The U.K's FTSE 100 added 0.5%.
  • Oil prices were headed for a modest weekly gain but concerns over OPEC+ supply and a potential Iran deal continue to weigh on sentiment. Brent crude and WTI were last flat at $64.56 and $61.63 a barrel, respectively, after dropping more than 2% in the previous session.
  • Gold prices are on track for a weekly loss of nearly 4% as easing trade tensions between the U.S. and China weaken demand for safe-haven assets. In early trading, futures fall 0.3% to $3,216.80 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar trades 0.2% higher on the week, making bullion more expensive for holders of other currencies.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Columbus Circle Capital Corp. I Prices Upsized $220 Million IPO
data.description:
Columbus Circle Capital Corp. I (CCCM) said late Thursday it priced its initial public offering of 22 million units at $10 each for $220 million, upsized from the previously planned $200 million.

Underwriters have a 45-day overallotment option to buy up to 3.3 million additional units.

Each unit consists of one class A share and half of a redeemable warrant, with each warrant exercisable at $11.50 per share.

The blank-check company expects the units to start trading on the Nasdaq Global Market under the symbol CCCMU Friday, while its shares and warrants are expected to be listed under the symbols CCCM and CCCMW, respectively.

Columbus Circle expects the IPO to close by Monday.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0724 GMT - Eutelsat's executives didn't rule an equity issue as they expect funding from export credit agencies won't be enough to cover its financing plan, Bryan Garnier's Antoine Lebourgeois says in a research note. The remarks came after the French satellite company reported third-quarter revenue that was slightly lower on year, but in line with expectations, the analyst says. "Management indicated ongoing work on a revised financing plan, with [export credit agencies] expected to cover most, though not all, of funding needs, and signalled openness to equity issuance," the analyst says. The company said in February it was working on a financing plan in line with its strategy and long-term objective for debt reduction. Shares fall 6.7% to 3.68 euros. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0532 GMT - Alibaba's cloud business revenue growth is likely to continue to accelerate, according to Daiwa analyst John Choi in a research note. Daiwa forecasts the company's cloud revenue to see an over 20% on-year growth in fiscal 1Q, following an 18% growth in the previous quarter. Despite the rise in research and development and other expenses, its cloud EBITA margin is likely to maintain a stable high-single-digit percentage margin in the coming quarters, with AI application demand continuing to rise, Daiwa says. The analyst keeps a buy rating and slightly cuts target price to HK$170.00 from HK$175.00. Shares are last traded at HK$123.30.(tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0337 GMT - Xero's record organic subscriber growth in North America supports the cloud-accounting software provider's decision to increase its focus on the U.S. market, its bulls at Goldman Sachs say. They write in a note that the investment makes sense, pointing out that growth in the last fiscal year came despite a subdued performance in Canada. They see reason to be confident in Xero's fiscal 2026 revenue guidance, and like the acceleration in Xero's revenue from payments. GS lifts Ebit forecasts for the next two fiscal years to reflect stronger revenue trends, raising its target price 2% to A$205.00. GS keeps a buy rating on the stock, which is down 0.2% at A$181.69. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0326 GMT - Growth in the lifetime value of Xero's customers can drive material share-price gains in the next two or three years, say its bulls at Morgan Stanley. The MS analysts tell clients in a note that they see lifetime value rising from NZ$17.95 billion in the accounting software provider's last fiscal year, to NZ$21.5 billion in fiscal 2026, and to NZ$25.8 billion in fiscal 2027. They don't think that the market is fully appreciating the scale of this rise, which they see as driving equity value. Xero's rule-of-40 performance, free cash-flow, and average-revenue-per-user growth in its last fiscal year all support MS's continued overweight rating and A$220.00 target price. Shares are down 0.1% at A$181.935. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0122 GMT - Gentrack's airports business, Veovo, has a long runway of growth, reckons Forsyth Barr. Revenue from providing airports with cloud-based software grew by 46% in FY 2024 to NZ$31.9 million, outpacing the 22% growth of its bigger utilities business. Analyst James Lindsay says this underscores Veovo's potential to contribute meaningfully to Gentrack's expansion, especially as airports world-wide face more pressure to modernize their systems. "We now estimate a global total addressable market for Veovo of NZ$1.6 billion--up 23% on our previous estimate--due to the expansion of Veovo's product suite and FX movements," Forsyth Barr says. It expects M&A to continue to play an important role in Veovo's growth. Forsyth Barr has an outperform call on Gentrack. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2400 GMT - Xero's bull at Barrenjoey stays positive on the cloud-accounting software provider after its cost outlook beat its expectations. Analyst Eric Choi expected fiscal 2026 expenses to be 72% of annual revenue, so Xero's guidance for a 71.5% expenses-to-revenue ratio supports improvements to his earnings forecasts. He expects Xero to exceed the so-called rule-of-40 metric in fiscal 2026 comfortably. Barrenjoey lifts its target price by 11% to A$200.00 and stays overweight on the stock. Shares are at A$182.05 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2329 GMT [Dow Jones]--Xero loses its bull at Jefferies on the cloud-accounting software provider's signal that it is placing an increased focus on revenue growth. Analyst Roger Samuel cuts his recommendation to hold from buy, telling clients in a note that Xero's introduction of the so-called rule-of-X into its performance metrics implies that it may sacrifice short-term free cash-flow margins. Samuel isn't concerned about Xero's investment discipline when it comes to driving growth, notably in the U.S., but he still sees downside risk to margins. Jefferies raises its target price 5.3% to A$194.80. Shares are at A$182.05 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2226 GMT - Appaloosa raised its Uber stake to 3.2 million shares from 1.5 million in the first quarter, while reducing its Lyft position to 9 million from 13.5 million. Elsewhere in Thursday's 13-F filings, Soros Fund Management reported that it reduced its Uber position by 265,667 shares, to 638,218. It also decreased holdings of another Uber competitor, DoorDash. Meanwhile, Maplelane Capital exited its Uber position. Uber surged in February as Bill Ackman disclosed a roughly $2.3 billion stake. Last week, Uber reported that first-quarter revenue rose 14%, to $11.5 billion. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

2054 GMT - Applied Materials isn't seeing any slowdown in demand, according to CFO Brice Hill, even as tariffs and macroeconomic headwinds threaten to rock many of its chip giant customers like Intel and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing. Applied Materials hasn't seen significant demand changes and is poised to navigate evolving trade conditions with its robust and diversified supply chain, Hill said. Applied Materials saw revenue rise 7% in the quarter, with sales in each of its segments up. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

2053 GMT - The Permian Basin, more known as a booming oil field, may surpass Northern Virginia as a data center hub, says David Capobianco, CEO of the private-equity firm Five Point Infrastructure. "We could literally create a hub in West Texas that can be massively built in excess of even the largest collection of data centers in [Northern] Virginia." He points to the Permian's large area as well as access to fiber networks, power sources and water produced from oil wells, which can be used to cool data-center equipment. Houston-based Five Point has backed several companies dedicated to support infrastructure development in the region. Those businesses include property manager LandBridge, water infrastructure operator WaterBridge and PowerBridge, a developer of data-center locations. (luis.garcia@wsj.com)

1953 GMT - Uber's new affordability-focused products make incremental market share gains more likely, even if they result in lower take-rates in the near term, UBS analysts say in a research note. The products will boost frequency with a focus on commuters, offering a more complete offering for such customers than rival Lyft. They will also help customer retention with more benefits for Uber One subscribers, which should lead to an increase in membership loyalty and more certainty around gross bookings trends in the long term, the analysts say. Uber's focus on affordability is especially timely as U.S. consumer sentiment slides, with risks to discretionary spending still a risk, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1732 GMT - Food delivery platforms appear to be expanding their scope beyond just eating in, helping consumers get--and get to-their restaurant reservations. During its annual product event, Uber shared more details around its OpenTable partnership, including a new "Dine Out" section in its Eats app that helps users book reservations. The partnership also gives discounts for rides to reservations booked with OpenTable, and lets users apply OpenTable loyalty points on Uber. The offerings come after DoorDash recently agreed to acquire reservation platform SevenRooms. Uber's deal could help offset that competition, and will also let the platform and its partners better track usage across channels, Bank of America Securities analysts say in a research note.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0738 GMT - In the absence of a U.S. recession, investors are upbeat about corporate earnings growth, Jochen Stanzl, chief market analyst at CMC Markets says in a note. "While company executives continue to emphasize growth risks, they are simultaneously presenting robust balance sheets to their shareholders," he says. Companies from sectors outside of automobiles, pharmaceuticals and energy--which are worst hit by tariffs--are navigating the trade policy challenges quite successfully. There, business is "thiving" and investors are leaning toward these sectors, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0124 GMT - Guidance will be a focus of Oceania Healthcare's FY 2025 result on Thursday, says Forsyth Barr. "Oceania Healthcare has failed to deliver operationally and financially for several years, and the market is losing faith in its care-focused business model," says analyst Aaron Ibbotson. It identifies three areas that Oceania Healthcare must deliver on in FY 2026 to regain confidence. Head-office costs must fall meaningfully. Net debt must reduce significantly. And Oceania Healthcare must have in place a plan to sell down its flagship Auckland village, The Helier. "We expect clear communication on all three issues and retain Outperform," Forsyth Barr says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1158 GMT - WELL Health Technologies' plan to sell off its US business would simplify the business and broaden investor appeal, TD Cowen's David Kwan says. The digital healthcare company says it intends to sell its US-based businesses in one-to-two years to free up more capital for its continued expansion in Canada, Kwan says. He calls the company's Canadian business growth impressive, and cites WELL's "highly accretive" M&A strategy. The analyst says WELL's plan to sell its U.S.-based businesses and redeploy capital into its Canadian business would allow the company to "gain a large war chest to buy back shares, repay debt and roll-up the Canadian clinic market in a highly accretive and capital-efficient way." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0834 GMT - Germany's Merck KGaA's results are mixed but positive momentum at its process solutions unit could help limit further downside to the stock, Stifel analysts say in a note. The German pharmaceutical company's process solutions division showed signs of recovery, growing organically by 11%, the analysts say. "Overall we find the LS PS progress encouraging, and see a path for the stock to find a floor," they say. Shares fall 5.6 % to 116.95 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0757 GMT - Standard Chartered's corporate and investment bank seems to be maintaining the good trajectory it has seen in the past five years, which was helped by leadership actions but also the period of rising rates and global volatility, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says. "It is often difficult to test management assertions... but it is clear that this business has outperformed in recent years," analysts Edward Firth and Elise Yu Ge write in a research note after a webinar with management. The business, which is key for the group as it brings in two-thirds of pretax profit, is betting on the deepening wallet share of large clients in a fragmented world for its next phase of growth. Shares in London have gained 16% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - Aegon's update feels a little messy overall but it won't likely move consensus expectations due to the one-off items, J.P.Morgan says in a note after the Dutch life insurer's first-quarter results. Analysts note the operating capital generation miss, the guidance retieration and the new 200 million-euro buyback, though it remains unclear whether there will be scope for another program this year. "The shares continue to look highly inexpensive to us; however, we are not sure if this update today will help close the valuation gap with peers," analysts write. Shares fall 1.2% to 6.14 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0746 GMT - Aegon's figures didn't particularly excite but its share buyback looks moderately supportive, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the Dutch insurer's first-quarter print. The group announced a 200 million euro buyback that will run until the end of 2025, on top of its current 150 million program, which will be completed next month. "We feel that consensus was more optimistic for the year (300 million-400 million euro range we think)," analyst Michele Ballatore writes. The run-rate of its holding cash position, which management is guiding for a normalized level of around 1 billion euros for the year, leaves some flexibility, he adds. Shares are down 1.1% at 6.14 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0703 GMT - Allianz has made steady progress recently which is already reflected in its share price, HSBC says in a research note. The German insurer's stock is trading above its one- and three-year averages and at a 3% premium to the sector. "We view Allianz's business model favorably with its strong balance sheet, diversification by business line and geography, as well as its focus on organic growth, productivity improvements, and improving economic value creation under its 2024-27 business plan," analysts write. However, they cut the rating on the stock to hold from buy as property and casualty headwinds are moderating and consensus already sits at the top end of management's guided range. Shares have risen by about a third over the year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0640 GMT - Swiss Re's first-quarter results were unusually benign when looking beyond the California wildfires, Jefferies says in a research note. "Although natural catastrophe losses cost Swiss Re's Property & Casualty Re business $570 million in 1Q 2025, far more than the seasonal budget of $363 million, this loss burden was still 20% below consensus, and thus a key driver behind the group beating consensus by 36%," analyst Philip Kett writes. Its life and health reinsurance claims also contributed to the better result. While insurance revenue across business lines looks somewhat light, there is little not to like about the print, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0638 GMT - Swiss Re profits beat consensus comfortably but this was driven by a higher investment result and lighter-than-expected natural catastrophe losses, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says after the reinsurer's first-quarter results. The combined ratio of its property and casualty reinsurance division was higher than consensus, which could be explained by the nominal catastrophe losses being lower than expectations despite being higher than what was budgeted. "Similarly to the other reinsurers, we think the market may be disappointed by the April renewals," analyst Darius Satkauskas writes in a research note. He also notes that though the headline price rose by 1.5%, the loss cost assumptions rose 3.7%, which leads to a negative 2.3% net price change. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0602 GMT - Assets under administration at U.K. investment platforms were roughly stable over the first quarter as the market yo-yoed against a volatile geopolitical backdrop, data compiled by Fundscape shows. Assets edged down 0.1% on quarter to 1.142 trillion pounds at the end of March. Gross flows were 46.9 billion pounds, while net flows amounted to 11.3 billion pounds--the best quarterly performance in three years, according to Fundscape's report. The top five platforms by net flows across all channels were Quilter, followed by AJ Bell, Hargreaves Lansdown, Aviva and Fidelity. "Harder to predict is what will happen in Q2--Trump's Liberation Day coincided with the last week of the tax year, so [individual savings accounts] activity is likely to have been hit hard," Fundscape CEO Bella Caridade-Ferreira says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0153 GMT - Insurance Australia Group aims to pay for two deals with Australian motoring clubs using existing capital, but Morgan Stanley sees little margin for error. IAG yesterday unveiled a deal worth A$1.35 billion to acquire the insurance business of The Royal Automobile Club of Western Australia, or RAC, and license its brand. That follows IAG's A$855 million deal in November with motoring club RACQ to expand its general-insurance business in Queensland. Analyst Andrei Stadnik says IAG's funding plan carries risks to dividend expectations and could lead to an equity raising. "Our forecasts have IAG generating A$700 million of capital assuming 70% dividend payout, the middle of the 60-80% target range or a A$50 million buffer," MS says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2226 GMT - Appaloosa raised its Uber stake to 3.2 million shares from 1.5 million in the first quarter, while reducing its Lyft position to 9 million from 13.5 million. Elsewhere in Thursday's 13-F filings, Soros Fund Management reported that it reduced its Uber position by 265,667 shares, to 638,218. It also decreased holdings of another Uber competitor, DoorDash. Meanwhile, Maplelane Capital exited its Uber position. Uber surged in February as Bill Ackman disclosed a roughly $2.3 billion stake. Last week, Uber reported that first-quarter revenue rose 14%, to $11.5 billion. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

2053 GMT - The Permian Basin, more known as a booming oil field, may surpass Northern Virginia as a data center hub, says David Capobianco, CEO of the private-equity firm Five Point Infrastructure. "We could literally create a hub in West Texas that can be massively built in excess of even the largest collection of data centers in [Northern] Virginia." He points to the Permian's large area as well as access to fiber networks, power sources and water produced from oil wells, which can be used to cool data-center equipment. Houston-based Five Point has backed several companies dedicated to support infrastructure development in the region. Those businesses include property manager LandBridge, water infrastructure operator WaterBridge and PowerBridge, a developer of data-center locations. (luis.garcia@wsj.com)

1814 GMT - Gold is steady at the $3,200 a troy ounce-mark, following a Wednesday slide. After a record-breaking first half of the year, analysts expect the second half of the year to be tougher and "provide ammunition to gold bears," says Hamad Hussain of Capital Economics in a note. But, Hussain predicts that gold will find new record-highs in 2026, citing "the structural factors which have been key drivers of the gold rally." Front-month gold climbs 1.2% to $3,220.70 per troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1726 GMT - Canadian home prices fell in April on a one-month and 12-month basis, but the gap between the cost of a residence and household income remains one of the widest in the developed world, says Carl Gomez, chief economist at CoStar Group's Canadian unit. Canadians were able to buy homes by loading up on debt during an extended period of rock-bottom rates. Canadian household debt as a share of GDP is now higher than it was in the US at the peak of America's housing bubble in 2008, Gomez says. Canada's housing affordability problem won't ease unless Canadians "see a significant increase in income and economic growth," Gomez says. "That won't happen over months. It will take years." (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0738 GMT - In the absence of a U.S. recession, investors are upbeat about corporate earnings growth, Jochen Stanzl, chief market analyst at CMC Markets says in a note. "While company executives continue to emphasize growth risks, they are simultaneously presenting robust balance sheets to their shareholders," he says. Companies from sectors outside of automobiles, pharmaceuticals and energy--which are worst hit by tariffs--are navigating the trade policy challenges quite successfully. There, business is "thiving" and investors are leaning toward these sectors, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0545 GMT - Manila Electric appears to lack any further rerating catalysts, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. Its share price has risen 18% year-to-date through May 14, thanks to recent announcements including franchise renewal, the analyst notes. Also, its share price looks a bit overvalued based on the current price-to-earnings and price-to-book-value ratios, the analyst says. Hence, the brokerage downgrades the stock's rating to hold from add. However, given SB Equities makes no adjustments to its current assumptions about the Philippine electric distribution utility, the brokerage maintains the target price at PHP562.80. Shares are 1.3% lower at PHP562.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1902 GMT - Oil futures post back-to-back losses as President Trump expresses optimism about reaching a nuclear deal with Iran, and the IEA forecasts a looser supply and demand balance this year despite a slight uptick in its demand growth forecast. Citi analysts nudged up their 0-3 month point price view for Brent to $60 on the cutback in U.S. and China tariffs earlier this week, but cited the probability of a U.S.-Iran nuclear deal in keeping its 2Q and 3Q averages at $62 and $63 a barrel, respectively. "Though if talks fall through and the situation escalates, oil could move to $70+," they add in a note. WTI settles down 2.4% at $61.62 a barrel, and Brent falls 2.4% to $64.53 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1604 GMT--With the summer high-demand season approaching and refineries ramping up, it would make sense for OPEC+ to make another accelerated output increase in July, says Homayoun Falakshahi, head crude analyst at Kpler. OPEC+ raised its production targets by 411,000 barrels a day in May and agreed on a similar amount for June. Despite two accelerated increases and expectations of another in July, "the impact on prices in the near term may not be that bearish," Falakshahi says. Another reason for OPEC+ increases could be a response to supply growth in the Atlantic basin, making that oil more expensive for Asia, he adds. Meanwhile, WTI and Brent are down 2.6% on President Trump's optimism about reaching a nuclear deal with Iran. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1312 GMT - Oil futures are lower after President Trump says the U.S. is getting close to a possible nuclear deal with Iran, which could lead to a lifting of sanctions on Iranian oil, while the market also digests a bearish monthly outlook from the International Energy Agency. The IEA nudged up its 2025 demand growth estimate by about 2% to 740,000 barrels a day, including growth of 650,000 b/d for the rest of the year, but also raised its supply growth forecast to 1.6 million b/d from 1.2 million b/d. Although recent trade deals eased pressure on crude prices, "increased trade uncertainty is expected to weigh on the world economy and, by extension, oil demand," the IEA said. WTI is down 2.8% at $61.36 a barrel, and Brent is off 2.7% at $64.29 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1008 GMT - Orsted is facing increasing risks to its U.S. offshore business, Berenberg analysts write. This follows the halt order issued to Equinor's U.S. Empire Wind project, which increases uncertainty about the delivery of Orsted's Revolution Wind and Sunrise Wind projects, the bank says. Proposed tax changes could also reduce potential growth in the U.S. onshore business. Elsewhere, Orsted's unexpected cancellation of the U.K. Hornsea 4 project and uncertainty related to the Polish Baltica 3 project raises questions about Orsted's medium-term growth. The bank lowers its price target to 250 Danish kroner from 390 kroner, no longer giving the company any credit for projects that have not reached a final investment decision. It downgrades the stock rating to hold from buy. Shares fall 2.9% to 244.30 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0909 GMT - Eni's plan to sell a 20% stake in Plentitude is consistent with its strategic plan and could allow the company to far exceed its 2025 divestment target, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. If the sale to Ares Management is finalized, Eni could exceed its 2 billion euros to 2.5 billion euros divestment target and help reduce its net-debt-to-equity ratio to around 16% by the end of the year, they write. This would be down from 22% in the fourth quarter of 2024 and 18% in the first quarter of 2025, they add. Shares trade down 0.8% at 13.33 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0841 GMT - Shares in European integrated energy companies trade lower on weaker oil prices and fears of a glut in supply. Brent crude dropped more than 3% to below $64 a barrel as data showed a surge in U.S. crude stockpiles, head of money and markets at Hargreaves Lansdown Susannah Streeter writes. Concerns that a U.S.-Iran deal could lead to a glut in supply have also pushed prices lower, she adds. In European morning trading, BP led the fallers, trading down nearly 4%, with British peer Shell dropping 2.7%. Norway's Equinor loses 2.2% while France's TotalEnergies and Portugal's Galp drop around 1.5%. Italy's Eni and Spain's Repsol trade down around 1%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)
